ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop (raw) class; no specific chump-cut lean variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers raw lamb meat broadly; no chump-specific lean sub-code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for lamb chump chop raw lean without risk of fabrication; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single lamb chump chop with bone, trimmed of all visible fat, weighs approximately 120 g and provides around 156 kcal, 27 g protein, and 5.2 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 in fat, raw
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Singapore HPB database values (energy 130 kcal, protein 22.5 g, fat 4.3 g, carbohydrate 0 g, sodium 65 mg, potassium 350 mg, calcium 6 mg, iron 3.1 mg, cholesterol 65 mg) are preserved as primary values per input provenance (HPB, confidence 0.95). USDA SR Legacy entry for lean raw lamb (FDC ID 174352) was used to cross-validate and to supply secondary nutrients not present in the HPB record (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, minerals). Where USDA values were used to supplement, this is noted. The chump cut is anatomically similar to the composite trimmed retail cuts entry; no exact chump-lean-raw entry exists in USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb chump chop raw lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. A numeric sentinel value of 0 is assigned per template numeric hygiene requirements. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (65 mg/100 g) and high potassium (350 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio supporting blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Lean lamb chump chop is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades, as its naturally low sodium and high potassium content align with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content produce no glycaemic impact; high protein (22.5 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean body mass
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean lamb chump chop is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate or sugar, exerts no glycaemic load, and provides high-quality protein that supports blood glucose stability when consumed as part of a balanced meal with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (65 mg/100 g) are present but at moderate levels for a lean red meat; trans fat is approximately 0.1 g/100 g (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat, primarily vaccenic acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean lamb chump chop may be consumed in moderate portions (up to 2–3 times per week) by individuals managing cardiovascular disease, as the low saturated fat content of this trimmed cut is within acceptable limits per AHA guidelines, but total red meat intake should be monitored and cooking methods should avoid added saturated fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in red meat may amplify inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA; low saturated fat reduces but does not eliminate this concern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit lamb consumption to occasional servings, prioritise anti-inflammatory proteins such as fatty fish, and if consuming lamb, pair with turmeric, ginger, or omega-3-rich foods to partially offset the pro-inflammatory effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb chump chop, raw, lean is a bone-in cut of raw lamb taken from the rear chump section of the carcass, with all separable fat removed, leaving only lean muscle meat. The meat displays a deep red colour with a fine marbled texture characteristic of young lamb. In its raw state it has a mild, slightly gamey aroma typical of lamb. When cooked, it develops a tender, juicy texture with a rich, savoury flavour. The chump is located between the loin and the leg, yielding a moderately thick, well-muscled chop that is popular in Australian, New Zealand, British, and Middle Eastern cuisines. It is commonly grilled, pan-fried, or oven-roasted. Nutritionally, per 100 g this lean cut provides approximately 130 kcal, 22.5 g of high-quality complete protein, 4.3 g of total fat (of which approximately 1.8 g is saturated), and zero carbohydrate or sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and high-protein dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 65 mg and potassium is high at 350 mg, supporting a favourable blood pressure profile consistent with DASH diet principles. Iron content is notable at 3.1 mg per 100 g (haem iron, highly bioavailable), making this cut a clinically relevant source for iron-deficiency prevention. Zinc (3.4 mg), vitamin B12 (2.6 mcg), and niacin (5.7 mg) are also well represented. The food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management when prepared without added salt or sugar-containing marinades, and may be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease. Those with rheumatoid arthritis should limit intake due to arachidonic acid content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database with confidence 0.95 and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lean raw lamb composite. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, B-vitamins, minerals) supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb muscle meat contains no digestible carbohydrate; confirmed zero per Singapore HPB database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure animal muscle food containing no plant fibre. Glycemic index is assigned numeric value 0 as a sentinel for not-applicable status in this template; gi_category is set to not_applicable and gi_source documents that this food contains negligible carbohydrates and no GI measurement is meaningful. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in the HPB record and USDA SR Legacy reports only retinol activity equivalents for composite lamb cuts with variable values; specific chump lean raw value not reliably available. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible vitamin D in lean raw lamb but no specific chump cut value is available. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB; USDA SR Legacy does not report vitamin E for this specific lean lamb cut. Trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (primarily vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid), not industrially produced; this is distinct from artificial trans fat and is noted for completeness. Saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (~1.7 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (~0.4 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite lean lamb raw entry (FDC ID 174352) as the HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions. The chump cut is anatomically proximate to the composite retail cuts entry used; values are considered a close estimate. Cultural classification as mamak in the input has been revised to Generic/Western as lamb chump chop is a generic raw ingredient cut, not a mamak dish; it may be used in mamak cooking but the food itself is not cuisine-specific.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name. This is a generic raw ingredient, not a Singaporean/Japanese/Korean/Indonesian/Thai/Vietnamese dish, so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6. G6 passes.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 130.0, protein_g 22.5, fat_g 4.3, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Sourced from Singapore HPB (confidence 0.95) cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0, sodium_mg 65.0, saturated_fat_g 1.8, sugar_g 0.0 all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 as sentinel value for not-applicable status (food contains negligible carbohydrates). gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0. gi_source populated with full explanation. Validator required glycemic_index to be numeric; 0 is used as the numeric sentinel consistent with template requirements and documented in notes and gi_source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for lamb chop raw (closest_parent match documented). FoodEx2 A00LC for lamb meat raw provided (closest_parent match documented). SNOMED CT left null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is Type 2 Diabetes.

All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array. No mandatory non-English aliases required as this is not a Singaporean/Japanese/Korean/Indonesian/Thai/Vietnamese dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON with HPB values preserved and USDA SR Legacy used for secondary nutrients.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent'. Ensured glycemic_index was null for negligible carbohydrate food.

Iteration 3: Validator feedback required glycemic_index to be numeric. Changed glycemic_index from null to 0 (numeric sentinel) as validator requires a numeric value. Updated gi_source and notes to document that 0 is a sentinel for not-applicable status, not a true GI measurement. gi_category remains not_applicable.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and all eight Q criteria. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value per validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is not_applicable and gi_source and notes fully document that this food contains negligible carbohydrates and no GI measurement applies. Core macros complete and sourced from Singapore HPB cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy. Zero macros justified as naturally zero for lean muscle meat. Health context covers all three required conditions. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias consistency verified. Score 8/8.
